summary: This abstract outlines the role of implementation science in adapting evidence-based interventions for population health. Colditz and Lobb argue that for the field to succeed, the scientific paradigm must shift to prioritize stakeholder input and external validity over traditional publication metrics.

principles.statement: Scientific paradigms must shift toward greater stakeholder input and improved reporting on external validity.
principles.rationale: To ensure research is relevant and provides actionable information that helps decision-makers select research-tested interventions to improve population health.
principles.source_quote: However, for implementation science to reach its full potential to improve population health the existing paradigm for how scientists create evidence, prioritize publications, and synthesize research needs to shift toward greater stakeholder input and improved reporting on external validity.

signature_quotes.text: However, for implementation science to reach its full potential to improve population health the existing paradigm for how scientists create evidence, prioritize publications, and synthesize research needs to shift toward greater stakeholder input and improved reporting on external validity.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the necessary evolution of research practices to make implementation science effective.

anti_patterns.description: Creating evidence, prioritizing publications, and synthesizing research without adequate stakeholder input or reporting on external validity.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents implementation science from reaching its full potential to improve population health and fails to provide relevant guidance for decision-makers.
